title: Thy Bosss Wife

jokes.text: have I ever told you that you have a magnificent telephone voice?
jokes.setup: Louie is clearly sucking up to his boss Mr. McKenzie on the phone
jokes.punchline: The obsequious compliment about his 'magnificent telephone voice' is absurdly specific flattery
jokes.explanation: Classic Louie - the specific absurdity of complimenting someone's 'telephone voice' while clearly brown-nosing is perfectly in character

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Yes, Mr. McKenzie. Yes, sir. You have a magnificent telephone voice, sir.

jokes.text: Uh, well, have I told you today?
jokes.setup: Continuation of the telephone voice compliment
jokes.punchline: Doubling down on the ridiculous flattery by asking if he's complimented it TODAY specifically
jokes.explanation: Good escalation of the sycophantic behavior, economical delivery

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Have I told you today how beautiful your voice is?

jokes.text: I was under the impression you liked it kissed.
jokes.setup: Louie apologizing for something to his boss
jokes.punchline: Reveals he thought McKenzie liked having his ass kissed - crude but perfectly Louie
jokes.explanation: Sharp punchline that perfectly captures Louie's crude directness and misreading of social situations

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: I'm sorry, boss. I thought McKenzie liked having his ass kissed.

dialogue.speaker: McKenzie
dialogue.line: What?!

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: You know, the attention, the compliments. I was just trying to keep him happy.

jokes.text: That's a man walking, all right.
jokes.setup: Bobby has just performed a mime exercise called 'Man Walking'
jokes.punchline: Elaine's dry confirmation that it does indeed look like a man walking
jokes.explanation: Mild deadpan humor, functional but not particularly clever

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: It looks like a man walking.

jokes.text: That's 'A Man Standing in Garage.'
jokes.setup: Tony explaining what his mime performance represented
jokes.punchline: The mundane, unimaginative concept of 'A Man Standing in Garage'
jokes.explanation: Funny in its complete lack of imagination or artistry - very Tony-like

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: The mundane, unimaginative concept of 'A Man Standing in Garage'

jokes.text: A duel of wits between unarmed opponents.
jokes.setup: Bobby and Tony's exchange of mime insults
jokes.punchline: Classic 'unarmed opponents' wordplay about their lack of intelligence
jokes.explanation: Well-delivered classic joke but not original - it's an old Oscar Wilde line

dialogue.speaker: Bobby
dialogue.line: You're so dumb, you couldn't pour water out of a boot if the instructions were on the heel.

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: Oh yeah? Well you're so stupid, you think a quarterback is a refund.

dialogue.speaker: Bobby
dialogue.line: That's nothing. You're so dumb, you went to the library to borrow a book about paranoia and nobody was there.

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: You think that's bad? You're so stupid, you sold your car for gas money.

jokes.text: When did they put that in here?
jokes.setup: Jim walks in and sees Bobby's mime performance
jokes.punchline: Jim thinks the invisible glass box is real and wonders when it was installed
jokes.explanation: Perfectly executed character moment - Jim's confusion is completely believable and funny, great structure building on the mime setup

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: When did we get this glass box installed?

dialogue.speaker: Bobby
dialogue.line: It's not real, Jim. I'm doing a mime routine.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Oh... well, you had me fooled.

jokes.text: Can he hear us in there?
jokes.setup: Jim's continued belief that Bobby is trapped in a real glass box
jokes.punchline: His concern about whether Bobby can hear them through the 'glass'
jokes.explanation: Good escalation of Jim's misunderstanding, stays true to his character

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Bobby! Bobby, can you hear me? Bobby!

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: I don't think he can hear us through the glass.

jokes.text: Ignatowski, why don't you try renting out that vacant lot between your ears?
jokes.setup: Jim's confusion about the mime
jokes.punchline: Louie's elaborate insult about Jim having empty space in his head
jokes.explanation: Classic Louie insult with good imagery, though not particularly original concept

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: You got so much empty space in your head, I could rent it out for a mime convention!

jokes.text: Okeydoke.
jokes.setup: Louie's harsh insult about Jim's intelligence
jokes.punchline: Jim's cheerful, oblivious 'Okeydoke' response
jokes.explanation: Perfect character moment - Jim's complete obliviousness to the insult is perfectly executed with maximum economy

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: You're dumber than a box of rocks.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Okeydoke!

jokes.text: that bloated gasbag would be escaping out the back window.
jokes.setup: Explaining why she wants to surprise her husband
jokes.punchline: Calling her husband a 'bloated gasbag' who would escape out the window
jokes.explanation: Good characterization and funny insult with vivid imagery

dialogue.speaker: Mrs. McKenzie
dialogue.line: He's a bloated gasbag who would escape out the window

jokes.text: I hope that you and that bloated gasbag patch up that little tiff you're having.
jokes.setup: Mrs. McKenzie just called her husband a bloated gasbag
jokes.punchline: Louie immediately adopts her insult while pretending to be diplomatic
jokes.explanation: Excellent immediate callback showing Louie's sycophantic nature and poor social skills

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Now Mrs. McKenzie, I'm sure your husband didn't mean to be a bloated gasbag.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: But between you and me, the man's a real bloated gasbag.

jokes.text: I picked up a touch of that in Mexico one summer.
jokes.setup: Louie mentions 'Mrs. McKenzie's revenge'
jokes.punchline: Jim thinks it's a disease he caught in Mexico
jokes.explanation: Perfect Jim moment - his innocent misunderstanding of the situation as a medical condition is very funny

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Mrs. McKenzie's revenge

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Is that what I caught in Mexico?

jokes.text: this sounds like one of your little sweaty fantasies.
jokes.setup: Louie's story about Mrs. McKenzie seducing cabbies
jokes.punchline: Alex's dismissive characterization of it as Louie's 'sweaty fantasy'
jokes.explanation: Great characterization - Alex's skeptical response with the perfect adjective 'sweaty' to describe Louie's fantasies

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: That's not a story, Louie. That's a sweaty fantasy.

jokes.text: I mean... he vanishes. Like he was never born.
jokes.setup: Question about whether the cabbie loses his job
jokes.punchline: Louie's ominous suggestion that they completely disappear
jokes.explanation: Excellent escalation with dark humor - the pause and delivery make this genuinely unsettling and funny

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: You want my advice? Forget about the job. We'll make it look like you never existed. We'll torch your apartment, drain your bank account, assume new identities in Buenos Aires.

jokes.text: either you or Wheeler are doomed. I'm hoping she notices Wheeler's got the better nose.
jokes.setup: Louie explaining that Mrs. McKenzie picks young, good-looking cabbies
jokes.punchline: His hope that she'll pick Wheeler because of his superior nose
jokes.explanation: Perfect Louie moment - his self-serving hope that someone else gets picked, with the oddly specific compliment about Wheeler's nose

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Maybe she'll pick Wheeler because of his superior nose.

jokes.text: he died with a smile on what was left of his face.
jokes.setup: Story about Curly Melnick's fate after being picked by Mrs. McKenzie
jokes.punchline: The darkly comic detail about smiling with 'what was left of his face'
jokes.explanation: Excellent dark humor with perfect economy - the phrase 'what was left of his face' is both disturbing and hilarious

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: So Mrs. McKenzie picks Curly Melnick. You know what happened to him? He's still smiling with what was left of his face.

jokes.text: Because if my fooling around with somebody halfway decent upsets my husband... this'll kill him.
jokes.setup: Louie asking why she picked him
jokes.punchline: Her explanation that picking someone decent would upset her husband, but picking Louie will kill him
jokes.explanation: Brutal and perfectly delivered insult - the logic is sound but devastating to Louie, excellent structure and economy

dialogue.speaker: Mrs. McKenzie
dialogue.line: If I picked someone decent, it would upset my husband. But you... you'll kill him.

jokes.text: Look, there doesn't have to be a Sheila... hold on. Hey, Sheila!
jokes.setup: Alex insisting there's no Sheila at his number to a persistent caller
jokes.punchline: Immediately calling out to Sheila who apparently does live there
jokes.explanation: Great contradiction comedy - Alex's insistence there's no Sheila immediately undercut by calling for her

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: There is no Sheila here. You have the wrong number. Please stop calling.

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: Sheila! Phone!

jokes.text: Yeah. Genetics.
jokes.setup: Louie asking why he looks like a mess
jokes.punchline: Alex's brutal one-word answer: genetics
jokes.explanation: Perfect economy and timing - one word delivers a devastating insult about Louie's appearance

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Why do you look like a mess?

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: Genetics.

jokes.text: Why would she pick you? Come on, admit it... You're no Curly Melnick.
jokes.setup: Alex questioning why Mrs. McKenzie would pick Louie
jokes.punchline: Pointing out he's not as attractive as the allegedly deceased Curly Melnick
jokes.explanation: Good callback to the earlier story with the dark implication about Curly's current state

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: Why would Mrs. McKenzie pick Louie?

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: He's not as attractive as the allegedly deceased Curly Melnick.

jokes.text: If I wasn't about to die, I'd be sick.
jokes.setup: Alex's comment about helping nice, deserving people
jokes.punchline: Louie's response that he'd be sick at the sentiment if he wasn't facing death
jokes.explanation: Perfect Louie response - even facing death, he can't help but be cynical about niceness

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: I'd be sick at the sentiment if I wasn't facing death.

jokes.text: There is no way in the world that you are going to seduce me. I want you!
jokes.setup: Louie's firm declaration of resistance to Mrs. McKenzie
jokes.punchline: His immediate contradiction with 'I want you!'
jokes.explanation: Perfect Louie moment - his complete lack of willpower immediately undermining his tough stance

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Mrs. McKenzie, I don't want you!

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: I want you!

jokes.text: He can't stand the sight of you. Then I'll have his woman.
jokes.setup: Louie praising Mr. McKenzie as a fine human being
jokes.punchline: Mrs. McKenzie pointing out McKenzie hates Louie, leading to his 'then I'll have his woman' response
jokes.explanation: Good escalation showing Louie's quick shift from loyalty to revenge when his feelings are hurt

dialogue.speaker: Mrs. McKenzie
dialogue.line: But Mr. McKenzie hates you.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Then I'll have his woman.

jokes.text: Both of them?
jokes.setup: Mrs. McKenzie complimenting Louie's eyes
jokes.punchline: Louie's surprised question about whether she likes both his eyes
jokes.explanation: Brilliant self-deprecating humor - Louie's genuine surprise that someone could like both his eyes suggests he's aware one might be better than the other

dialogue.speaker: Mrs. McKenzie
dialogue.line: You have such beautiful eyes, Mr. De Palma.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: You like both of them?

jokes.text: That's 'Pleasuree.' It's very expensive. Oh, it should be. Poor people shouldn't smell like that.
jokes.setup: Discussion of Mrs. McKenzie's expensive perfume
jokes.punchline: Louie's observation that poor people shouldn't smell so good
jokes.explanation: Perfect class-conscious Louie observation with good economic structure

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: You know what your problem is, Mrs. McKenzie? Poor people shouldn't smell this good. It's bad for the economy.

jokes.text: Your palms are getting little sweat marks all over my gown.
jokes.setup: Louie's nervous resistance to going to the bedroom
jokes.punchline: The gross detail about his sweaty palms ruining her gown
jokes.explanation: Good cringe humor with specific physical detail that makes Louie's nervousness viscerally real

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: I don't know, Mrs. McKenzie. I'm a little nervous.

dialogue.speaker: Mrs. McKenzie
dialogue.line: Nervous? What's there to be nervous about?

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Well, my palms get all sweaty when I'm nervous.

dialogue.speaker: Mrs. McKenzie
dialogue.line: So?

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: So I'll ruin your gown.

jokes.text: If we lose each other, I'll meet you at the headboard.
jokes.setup: Louie commenting on the size of Mrs. McKenzie's bed
jokes.punchline: His suggestion they might get lost and need a meeting point
jokes.explanation: Excellent visual comedy line that perfectly captures the scale of a rich person's bedroom from Louie's perspective

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: You know, Mrs. McKenzie, that bed of yours is so big, you and your husband might get lost in there. You ought to establish a meeting point.

jokes.text: I have a war wound. What war? I can't remember. I had my memory shot off.
jokes.setup: Louie's desperate excuse about having a war wound
jokes.punchline: His explanation that he can't remember which war because his memory was shot off
jokes.explanation: Brilliant absurdist excuse - the illogical logic of having his memory shot off is perfectly Louie

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: I got a war wound, see? That's why I can't remember things.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: I can't remember which war it was because my memory was shot off.

jokes.text: All I want to do is go to bed.
jokes.setup: Mr. McKenzie has just returned home where his wife and Louie are in the bedroom
jokes.punchline: His innocent statement about wanting to go to bed, unaware of what's happening there
jokes.explanation: Good situational irony - his innocent desire creates dramatic tension given what's happening in the bedroom

dialogue.speaker: Mr. McKenzie
dialogue.line: I'm exhausted. I think I'll go to bed.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Not now, you can't!

dialogue.speaker: Mr. McKenzie
dialogue.line: Why not? I'm tired.

jokes.text: Remember the last time we kissed and made up like this? It took us three whole days.
jokes.setup: The McKenzies deciding to make up after their fight
jokes.punchline: McKenzie's fond memory of their three-day reconciliation, unaware Louie is hiding nearby
jokes.explanation: Situational comedy with the dramatic irony of McKenzie's romantic plans while Louie is trapped

dialogue.speaker: Mr. McKenzie
dialogue.line: Remember that wonderful three-day reconciliation we had after our big fight?

dialogue.speaker: Mrs. McKenzie
dialogue.line: Oh yes, darling. Those were the most beautiful days of our marriage.

dialogue.speaker: Mr. McKenzie
dialogue.line: We made love on the kitchen table, the living room sofa, and even in the bathtub.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: That was me.

jokes.text: I may be slow, but once I catch it, it sticks.
jokes.setup: Jim finally understanding that Bobby's mime exercises are not real objects
jokes.punchline: His self-aware comment about his slow comprehension but good retention
jokes.explanation: Nice callback to the opening mime confusion with Jim's characteristic self-awareness about his limitations

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: I may be slow, but I never forget.

jokes.text: Bobby, when the hell are you going to get this thing out of here?
jokes.setup: Jim now understanding that the mime box isn't real
jokes.punchline: But he's still referring to removing 'this thing' as if it's a real object
jokes.explanation: Perfect callback showing Jim still doesn't fully understand - he gets it's not real but still wants it 'removed'

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Wait, so the box isn't real?

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: But I still can't get this thing off me.

tmdb_overview: At the garage, the cabbies overhear a screaming match between the company's owner, Mr. McKenzie, and his wife. Meanwhile, Louie tells them that whenever McKenzie and his wife are fighting, she chooses a handsome young cab driver to have a fling with, then confesses to her husband, and the cabbie is then never heard from again. Louie continues that even if the cabbie turns her down, she still will make up a story about him to tell her husband. The cabbies don't buy it until Alex comes in, overhears Mrs. McKenzie about to come downstairs, and hides in his locker. Louie approaches Mrs. McKenzie, surveying the cab drivers in the garage, with vigor, hoping to pimp Tony or Bobby, but she decides on Louie (her explanation is that since her husband would be upset by her fooling around with a good looking man, being with Louie would kill him). Louie breaks into Alex's apartment while he's sleeping and begs him for advice and all Alex can come up with is that he confront her and try to talk to h
insight: Louie's affair subplot drives 85.9 score on character comedy alone without standout zingers.
